metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt` (with usage text for `rebar audit show <ticket> [--output json]`) to the Scope section, since `known_subcommands()` discovers subcommands dynamically from package-data .txt files — a code-only change to `_help.py` is insufficient.
findings.finding: The plan instructs to 'add "audit" to the set returned by src/rebar/_cli/_help.py::known_subcommands', but `known_subcommands()` is NOT a hardcoded set — it dynamically discovers subcommands by scanning package-data *.txt files under `rebar._cli.help/` (iterdir filter on .txt). Adding 'audit' to that set therefore requires creating a `src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt` package-data file, which the plan does not mention. Without that file, `known_subcommands()` will never return 'audit', and `main()` will reject `rebar audit show` as an unknown subcommand before reaching `_dispatch`.
findings.location: Scope section — 'CLI registration' paragraph; src/rebar/_cli/_help.py::known_subcommands (lines 57-67)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope bullet: 'also create `src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt` with usage text for `rebar audit show`, since `known_subcommands()` reads subcommand names from package-data *.txt files, not a hardcoded set.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Spell out in the Scope section that `src/rebar/audit/read.py` must compose BOTH the PASS attestation (from `state['attestations']['completion-verifier']` via `rebar.show`) AND the FAIL sidecar record (via `llm.completion_sidecar.latest_fail_verdict`) into the `completion` field, and define the merge shape.
findings.finding: The plan states the `completion` field of `AuditTrail` holds 'the completion attestation plus any completion sidecar record', but these are stored in two entirely different locations with different readers: (a) a PASS completion stores a cryptographic attestation in `state['attestations']['completion-verifier']` (reducer-compiled, read via `rebar.show` / `signing.py`); (b) a FAIL completion stores a COMPLETION_VERDICT sidecar event (reducer-IGNORED, read via `llm.completion_sidecar.latest_fail_verdict`). The plan's `audit_trail` aggregator in `src/rebar/audit/read.py` must compose both sources, but the plan never names `completion_sidecar.latest_fail_verdict` or describes how the two sources are merged into a single `dict | None`. The implementation spec is underspecified at the reader-composition level.
findings.location: Data contract section — `completion` field definition; Scope section — `src/rebar/audit/read.py`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt` (and optionally `audit-show.txt`) to the Scope section so `known_subcommands()` can enumerate it and `rebar audit --help` / `rebar help audit` resolves correctly.
findings.finding: The plan instructs adding 'audit' to the set returned by known_subcommands() but omits that this is achieved by adding a help text file src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt — the mechanism known_subcommands() uses is exclusively enumerating .txt files in that package-data directory. No audit.txt (or audit-show.txt) is listed in the Scope section, and without it the 'audit' intercept would still be rejected by the unknown-subcommand guard for the rebar help audit path.
findings.location: Scope section: 'CLI registration' paragraph; src/rebar/_cli/_help.py::known_subcommands
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt` to the Scope list with a note that it contains the pinned help text for the `audit` subcommand (analogous to existing help .txt files), since known_subcommands() derives its set by enumerating that directory.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py` (specifically `latest_fail_verdict`) to the Scope section as a named read dependency of the `audit_trail` aggregator, clarifying how the two sources (signed attestation via `verify_signature` + FAIL sidecar via `latest_fail_verdict`) are composed into the `completion` field.
findings.finding: The plan's Scope section does not name src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py, but the AuditTrail.completion field ('completion attestation plus any completion sidecar record') must compose from it: the module holds latest_fail_verdict() which reads COMPLETION_VERDICT sidecar events (completion FAIL records). The read.py aggregator will implicitly depend on this module with no call-site named in the plan.
findings.location: Scope section; Data contract section: 'completion: dict | None  # completion attestation + sidecar record'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct 'rebar.show' to 'rebar.show_ticket' in the Approach rationale (and anywhere else the plan refers to it) to match the actual public library API.
findings.finding: The Approach rationale and Data contract both reference 'rebar.show' as the function used by the aggregator ('reusing rebar.show / rebar.list_tickets'), but the actual public library API is rebar.show_ticket; there is no rebar.show in the library namespace.
findings.location: Approach rationale section: 'reusing rebar.show / rebar.list_tickets'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `rebar.show` with `rebar.show_ticket` in the Approach rationale section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the internal dict shape of the non-None `completion` value — e.g. `{attestation: dict|None, fail_sidecar: dict|None}` — and update the integration-test oracle to assert at least one sub-key so the contract is verifiable.
findings.finding: The `completion` field in `AuditTrail` is described as 'completion attestation + sidecar record' but these originate from two distinct, disjoint sources — the `state['attestations']['completion-verifier']` record (present only on a PASS close) and the `completion_sidecar.latest_fail_verdict()` record (present only on a FAIL run) — and the plan does not specify the internal dict shape of the non-None `completion` value or how the two sources are merged/disambiguated. An implementer has no contract to verify against, making the integration-test AC ('completion is a dict') untestable without guessing the shape.
findings.location: Data contract section — `completion: dict | None` field description; Acceptance Criteria — 'that `completion` is a dict'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the data contract: `completion` when non-None is `{'attestation': dict|None, 'fail_sidecar': dict|None}` where `attestation` is `state['attestations'].get('completion-verifier')` and `fail_sidecar` is `completion_sidecar.latest_fail_verdict(ticket_id)`. The oracle should assert `completion['attestation'] is not None or completion['fail_sidecar'] is not None`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the scope instruction: instead of 'add `"audit"` to the set returned by `known_subcommands`', specify that the implementer must create `src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt` (the per-subcommand usage text) — this is the only mechanism by which `known_subcommands()` recognises a new subcommand.
findings.finding: The plan instructs the implementer to 'add `"audit"` to the set returned by `src/rebar/_cli/_help.py::known_subcommands`', but `known_subcommands()` is not a code-modified set — it auto-derives from `.txt` package-data files by scanning `rebar/_cli/help/` for `*.txt` filenames (excluding `overview.txt`). There is no code to modify; the only required action is creating `src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt`. Without this file the `main()` guard (`sub not in _help.known_subcommands()`) will reject `rebar audit` with 'unknown subcommand'.
findings.location: Scope section — 'add `"audit"` to the set returned by `src/rebar/_cli/_help.py::known_subcommands`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'add `"audit"` to the set returned by `src/rebar/_cli/_help.py::known_subcommands`' with 'create `src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt` with the usage text for `rebar audit show`; `known_subcommands()` auto-derives from this file.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Scope section's description of CLI registration: replace 'add `"audit"` to the set returned by `src/rebar/_cli/_help.py::known_subcommands`' with 'create `src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt` with the pinned help text for `rebar audit show`' (since `known_subcommands()` discovers subcommands from that directory's `.txt` files, not from a hardcoded set).
findings.finding: The plan states 'add `"audit"` to the set returned by `src/rebar/_cli/_help.py::known_subcommands`', but `known_subcommands()` is NOT a hardcoded set — it auto-discovers subcommand names by listing `*.txt` files in `src/rebar/_cli/help/`. The correct registration mechanism is to create `src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt` (a pinned help text file), not to modify `known_subcommands()` in code.
findings.location: Scope section: 'add `"audit"` to the set returned by `src/rebar/_cli/_help.py::known_subcommands` so it is a recognized subcommand'
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace: 'add `"audit"` to the set returned by `src/rebar/_cli/_help.py::known_subcommands`'
With: 'add `src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt` with the pinned `rebar audit show` help text (which `known_subcommands()` auto-discovers by listing `*.txt` files in `src/rebar/_cli/help/`)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exact key vocabulary and sources for the `completion` dict in `AuditTrail` — specify which fields come from `state['attestations']['completion-verifier']` (the HMAC/op-cert attestation record) and which from the `COMPLETION_VERDICT` sidecar payload, or define a single merged schema with explicit key names.
findings.finding: The `completion` field of `AuditTrail` is typed `dict | None` but the key vocabulary of the merged dict is never defined. The plan describes it as 'completion attestation + any completion sidecar record', which conflates two structurally different data sources: (a) `state['attestations']['completion-verifier']` — a cryptographic record with `signature`, `manifest`, `kind`, and envelope fields (from `signing.py`); and (b) `COMPLETION_VERDICT` sidecar events — `completion_verifier_fail_v1` payloads with `verdict`, `findings`, `remediation`, `certifiable` fields (from `completion_sidecar.py`). An implementer building `audit_trail()` cannot determine how to merge these, which fields to keep from each, or what the returned dict's keys are — making the schema underspecified at the one point where two heterogeneous sources must be unified.
findings.location: Data contract section — `completion: dict | None` field definition
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `CompletionRecord` TypedDict (or at minimum a prose key-table) to the Data contract section listing the exact fields returned: e.g., `attestation: dict | None` (the raw record from `state['attestations']['completion-verifier']`) and `sidecar: dict | None` (the `completion_verifier_fail_v1` payload from `latest_fail_verdict()`), keeping them nested rather than merged to avoid key collisions.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the CLI registration instruction: the mechanism for making `audit` a recognized subcommand is creating `src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt` (the pinned help text), NOT modifying `known_subcommands()` which is a dynamic file enumerator — align with how all other `.txt`-backed subcommands are registered.
findings.finding: The plan instructs to 'add `"audit"` to the set returned by `src/rebar/_cli/_help.py::known_subcommands`', but `known_subcommands()` has no hardcoded set — it dynamically enumerates `.txt` files from `src/rebar/_cli/help/`. The correct mechanism is creating `src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt` (the help text file). Other intercept-only commands that deliberately skip this step (`explain`, `verify-commit-ticket`) have no `.txt` file and are not in `known_subcommands()`. The plan's instruction misidentifies the registration mechanism, which could cause an implementer to modify `known_subcommands()` (adding a hardcoded string) or leave `audit` unrecognized when invoked after the intercept guard.
findings.location: Scope section — CLI registration paragraph; `src/rebar/_cli/_help.py::known_subcommands`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'add "audit" to the set returned by `src/rebar/_cli/_help.py::known_subcommands`' with 'create `src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt` with the pinned help text for `rebar audit show <ticket> [--output json]` — this is what makes `known_subcommands()` include `audit` (the function enumerates `.txt` files dynamically)'. The intercept in `main()` should then dispatch before the `known_subcommands` guard is reached, exactly as `workflow` / `llm` / `criteria` do.

findings.finding: The plan's implementation description for resolving code-review links is technically incorrect in a way that would cause an implementer to traverse the wrong data field and find zero results. The plan states 'resolving code reviews via the ticket's inbound relates_to links from code_review tickets.' In reality, emit_code_review_artifact calls rebar.link(artifact_id, work_ticket_id, 'relates_to') (voter.py:278) — the code_review artifact is the link SOURCE. Because relates_to is symmetric, graph/_links.py:264-267 also writes a reciprocal event in the work ticket's directory, giving the work ticket an OUTGOING dep to the artifact. But reducer/_inbound.py:114 explicitly suppresses inbound_links entries for symmetric relations when the subject already has that outgoing dep (own_symmetric check). An implementer reading the plan who calls find_inbound_relationships() or inspects show_ticket()['inbound_links'] for code_review entries will find nothing; the correct traversal is via the work ticket's outgoing deps (show_ticket(work_ticket_id)['deps']) filtered to relation=='relates_to', then resolving ticket_type=='code_review'.
findings.location: Approach rationale: 'resolving code reviews via the ticket's inbound relates_to links from code_review tickets'; Scope section: 'resolving code reviews via the ticket's relates_to links (reusing rebar.show / rebar.list_tickets)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope and Approach rationale sections, replace 'resolving code reviews via the ticket's inbound relates_to links from code_review tickets' with: 'resolving code reviews via the work ticket's outgoing deps list (show_ticket(ticket_id)["deps"]) filtered to relation=="relates_to", then selecting entries whose resolved ticket has ticket_type=="code_review" — the symmetric write in graph/_links.py ensures the work ticket carries an outgoing dep to the artifact, but reducer/_inbound.py suppresses the inbound_links entry for symmetric relations when the outgoing dep already exists.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the CLI registration contradiction: decide whether `audit` is a pure intercept (like `workflow`/`llm` — no help/.txt, not in known_subcommands, intercepted before the unknown-subcommand guard) or a dispatched command (like `show`/`list` — requires audit.txt in help/, a _dispatch arm, and no redundant intercept); update the plan to specify ONE consistent pattern with the correct wiring steps.
findings.finding: The plan contradicts itself on CLI registration: it says to follow the 'owns-its-own---help intercept pattern used by _workflow / _llm / _criteria' AND to 'add audit to the set returned by known_subcommands'. These are mutually exclusive: _workflow, _llm, and _criteria are pure intercepts in main() with NO .txt help file and are NOT in known_subcommands(); known_subcommands() is driven solely by .txt file presence in _cli/help/, not by any hand-edited set. If audit is a pure intercept (like workflow), it must NOT appear in known_subcommands() and needs NO help .txt file. If it IS in known_subcommands(), it also needs a dispatch arm in _dispatch() — which is absent from the plan. The plan must pick one pattern and be consistent.
findings.location: Scope section — 'CLI registration' paragraph; _cli/__init__.py::main intercept description; _cli/_help.py::known_subcommands note
findings.suggested_fix: Follow the pure-intercept pattern (matching _workflow/_llm/_criteria): add `if argv and argv[0] == 'audit': return _audit(argv[1:])` BEFORE the unknown-subcommand guard in main(), with no help/.txt file and no _dispatch arm. Remove the instruction to 'add audit to known_subcommands'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the concrete schema-acceptance predicate for `all_review_results()` — e.g., 'include any payload whose `schema` field starts with `plan_review_result_v` (prefix match for forward-compat)' or 'include only `plan_review_result_v1` now; note this is the extensibility point for future versions'.
findings.finding: The plan says `all_review_results(ticket)` enumerators should be 'schema-tolerant, accepting both v1 and v2 records', but no v2 schema exists for either `plan_review_result_v1` or `code_review_result_v1`. More importantly, the acceptance predicate for 'schema-tolerant' enumeration is never defined — should the enumerator include all readable payloads regardless of schema string, include only `v1` payloads, or include any payload whose `schema` starts with the right prefix? The existing `latest_review_result` reader accepts ONLY exact `plan_review_result_v1` — strictly applying the same predicate in `all_review_results` would silently drop any future-versioned sidecars, while dropping the schema guard entirely would include malformed/foreign payloads.
findings.location: Scope section — `all_review_results(ticket)` description in `plan_review/sidecar.py` and `code_review/sidecar.py`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'reusing rebar.show / rebar.list_tickets' with a concrete mechanism for inbound relates_to traversal — either name rebar.reducer._inbound.find_inbound_relationships as the primitive, or describe the list_tickets(ticket_type='code_review') scan approach explicitly, noting its O(N) cost.
findings.finding: The plan asserts code reviews will be resolved 'via the ticket's inbound relates_to links (reusing rebar.show / rebar.list_tickets)', but neither rebar.show nor rebar.list_tickets exposes inbound_links — this is explicitly confirmed by a code comment ('show emits the SAME compiled-state shape as list/search; the production dispatcher's show arm never augmented with inbound_links'). The TicketState TypedDict also has no inbound_links field. The actual internal API for inbound-link traversal is rebar.reducer._inbound.find_inbound_relationships, which is not mentioned in the plan.
findings.location: Scope section — 'New src/rebar/audit/read.py' bullet; 'Approach rationale' section
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, replace the parenthetical with: 'resolving code reviews via rebar.reducer._inbound.find_inbound_relationships(ticket_id, tracker_dir), filtering the resulting inbound_links for relation=="relates_to" entries whose from_id is a code_review ticket'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Evaluate `rebar.find_inbound_relationships` (already exported from `rebar.__init__`) as the primary mechanism for resolving inbound `relates_to` links in `audit_trail()`, rather than scanning via `rebar.list_tickets`; document in the Scope section why `list_tickets` is preferred if it is, or update the approach to use the canonical primitive.
findings.finding: The plan proposes to resolve inbound `relates_to` code-review links by 'reusing `rebar.show` / `rebar.list_tickets`', but the codebase already exports `rebar.find_inbound_relationships` (canonical public API in `rebar.reducer._inbound`, re-exported from `rebar.__init__`) which answers exactly this query — 'which tickets point at this one via relates_to?' — without a full-corpus list-and-filter scan. Not mentioning or evaluating this existing primitive risks the aggregator reinventing the inbound-link traversal.
findings.location: Scope section / `src/rebar/audit/read.py` description: 'resolving code reviews via the ticket's `relates_to` links (reusing `rebar.show` / `rebar.list_tickets`)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, replace 'reusing `rebar.show` / `rebar.list_tickets`' with 'reusing `rebar.find_inbound_relationships` to discover inbound `relates_to` links from `code_review` tickets, then `rebar.show` per linked ticket to read its sidecar history'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the partial-failure policy for `relates_to` resolution: either (a) skip unresolvable links and emit a warning log entry per skipped link, or (b) surface a structured error; add a corresponding AC or implementation note.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify behavior for partial failures during `relates_to` resolution — e.g., when fetching one of several linked `code_review` tickets fails mid-enumeration. The aggregator could silently drop that entry or raise and abort the entire call, neither of which is specified.
findings.location: Scope / `src/rebar/audit/read.py` — 'resolving code reviews via the ticket's `relates_to` links'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach rationale or Scope section: 'If fetching a linked code_review ticket raises an exception, log a warning (ticket_id, error) and include a `{"ticket_id": id, "error": "<msg>", "sidecars": []}` sentinel in `code_reviews` so callers can detect the gap.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define and test the error contract for `audit_trail` when the ticket_id is not found: specify whether it raises a typed exception (e.g., `TicketNotFoundError`) or returns a sentinel, and add an AC assertion for the not-found case.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify what `audit_trail` returns or raises when the ticket_id does not exist (e.g., unknown ticket). Without an explicit contract, implementations may swallow the error and return a misleading empty/partial result, or raise an unhandled exception that surfaces poorly in the CLI/MCP layer.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria / Data contract section / `src/rebar/audit/read.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a line to the data-contract section: 'If the ticket does not exist, `audit_trail` raises `TicketNotFoundError` (subclass of `ValueError`).' Add a corresponding AC: 'Oracle: calling `audit_trail("nonexistent-id")` raises `TicketNotFoundError`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify non-happy-path behavior for the CLI and MCP tool: define the exit code and error message format when `ticket_id` does not exist, when a linked code_review ticket is inaccessible, and add an AC oracle for at least the not-found case (e.g., non-zero exit + JSON `{"error": ...}` on stderr or a structured exception for MCP).
findings.finding: The CLI surface (`rebar audit show <ticket>`) specifies only the happy-path output format and the bare-ticket (empty-data) case; it does not specify error states: invalid/non-existent ticket_id, inaccessible relates_to-linked tickets, or storage failures — including exit codes and whether errors go to stderr or stdout.
findings.location: CLI registration section / AC line: `rebar audit show <ticket> --output json prints the same structure`
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the CLI spec: exit 0 on success, exit 1 on ticket-not-found (with a JSON `{"error": "ticket <id> not found"}` to stdout when `--output json` is set), and exit 2 on unexpected errors. For the MCP tool, raise a typed exception (or return `{"error": ...}`) when the ticket_id is invalid. Add one AC oracle: `rebar audit show <nonexistent-id> --output json` exits non-zero and emits a parseable error payload.

findings.finding: The plan names `rebar audit show <ticket> [--output json]` as a new CLI command but does not enumerate the required edit targets for CLI wiring: (1) an intercept arm in `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` (all existing custom subcommands like `review`, `review-code`, `workflow`, `enrich`, etc. each have an explicit `if argv and argv[0] == '<subcmd>':` intercept there), and (2) a help file `src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt` (required for `known_subcommands()` to recognize `audit` and for `rebar audit --help` to work).
findings.location: Scope section — 'CLI `rebar audit show <ticket> [--output json]`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: '- `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` — add intercept `if argv and argv[0] == "audit":` routing to the new audit CLI handler, following the pattern of `review`, `enrich`, etc.' and '- `src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt` — help text for `rebar audit show`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the internal key spec for `completion` to the data contract (e.g., `{attestation: dict, sidecar: dict | None}` or equivalent) so the executor has no design decision to make.
findings.finding: The `completion` field's internal dict structure is unspecified — the plan documents the outer TypedDict keys precisely but leaves the keys inside the `completion` dict (when non-None) for the executor to discover from existing attestation/sidecar code, forcing a design decision the plan should resolve.
findings.location: Data contract section — `completion: dict | None` row of AuditTrail TypedDict
findings.suggested_fix:
Extend the TypedDict comment for `completion` to show its sub-keys, for example:
```python
completion: dict | None  # None if no record; else {"attestation": dict, "sidecar": dict | None}
                         # where attestation is the raw completion-attestation event and
                         # sidecar is the latest completion-sidecar record (or None)
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC #1) that explicitly asserts `all_review_results(ticket)` is importable from both sidecar modules and returns a newest-first list when called directly, separately from the `audit_trail` aggregator test.
findings.finding: The `all_review_results(ticket)` enumeration helpers (to be added to `plan_review/sidecar.py` and `code_review/sidecar.py`) are named in Scope as explicit deliverables but have no acceptance criterion that directly verifies them — they are only exercised implicitly through the `audit_trail` integration test in AC #1. If an implementer ships `audit_trail` by inlining the history logic instead of adding the named helpers, all ACs pass but the described deliverable is absent.
findings.location: Scope section (sidecar files) vs Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC #1 with a sub-bullet: 'and that `all_review_results` imported from `rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar` (and `rebar.llm.code_review.sidecar`) returns a non-empty newest-first list when called on a ticket seeded with multiple REVIEW_RESULT events.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a sub-bullet to AC #2 verifying that `rebar audit --help` exits 0 and its output includes `audit` (i.e., the subcommand is recognized), confirming the `known_subcommands` and intercept wiring.
findings.finding: The Scope explicitly names two registration changes — adding `'audit'` to `known_subcommands` in `src/rebar/_cli/_help.py` and wiring the intercept in `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py::main` — but no AC verifies that `rebar audit --help` works, that `audit` is recognized as a known subcommand, or that the intercept dispatch is present. AC #2 only tests the happy-path JSON output of `rebar audit show <ticket> --output json`.
findings.location: Scope section (CLI registration) vs AC #2
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC #2: 'and that `rebar audit --help` exits 0 and its stdout contains the word "audit", confirming the known_subcommands registration.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add ACs (or extend AC1/AC2) covering: (a) `audit_trail` behavior for a nonexistent ticket_id (specify raises KeyError/returns None/raises RebarNotFound — pick and test it); (b) `rebar audit show` exit code and stderr for a nonexistent ticket; (c) `audit_trail` gracefully handles a dangling `relates_to` link (e.g., deleted code_review ticket); (d) `all_review_results` is schema-tolerant in practice by including a test with a malformed sidecar record.
findings.finding: The ACs cover only the happy path and the empty/bare-ticket path for `audit_trail()` and `rebar audit show`; there are no acceptance criteria for failure paths such as a nonexistent ticket_id, a ticket_id that resolves but has a dangling `relates_to` link to a deleted code_review ticket, or a malformed sidecar record encountered by the new `all_review_results` enumerators. For a new user-facing CLI command and library function, omitting failure-path ACs means broken error behavior can ship undetected.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all four AC bullets); Scope — `all_review_results` enumerators
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: '…plus assertions that `audit_trail("nonexistent")` raises `RebarTicketNotFound` (or equivalent) and that a ticket with a dangling `relates_to` code_review link returns `code_reviews` with the resolvable entries only.' Add to AC2: '…and that `rebar audit show nonexistent-ticket` exits non-zero with a human-readable error on stderr.' Add a note under the `all_review_results` scope item: 'Unit test feeds a list with one malformed record and asserts the valid records are returned and the malformed one is skipped (or logged) without raising.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Instead of new `all_review_results()` functions, confirm whether the existing `latest_review_result` / `_latest_payload_with_ts` helpers can be extended with an `all_results=True` parameter (or similar) to return the full history list — eliminating the duplicated directory-walk logic and the Rule-of-Three miss.
findings.finding: The proposed `all_review_results(ticket)` helpers in `plan_review/sidecar.py` and `code_review/sidecar.py` duplicate an already-present file-walk pattern (os.listdir + suffix filter + reversed + json.load + schema-guard) that exists in `latest_review_result` and `_latest_payload_with_ts` in those same files, with only one declared call-site each (`audit_trail` in the new `read.py`). This violates Rule-of-Three and is a mild NIH-within-module: the same traversal logic appears three times per file rather than being parameterized.
findings.location: Scope section — `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py` and `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` — 'add `all_review_results(ticket)` history enumerators'
findings.suggested_fix: In `plan_review/sidecar.py`, add an `all_review_results(ticket_id, *, repo_root=None) -> list[dict]` that calls the existing walk logic refactored into a shared `_walk_sidecar_files` helper also used by `latest_review_result`. Or, simpler: have `all_review_results` call the existing `_walk` function and collect all valid payloads instead of returning the first — one shared walk, two thin wrappers.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a note to CLAUDE.md (or an ADR) documenting the `src/rebar/audit/` read-layer pattern: its role as the single aggregator over plan-review / completion / code-review histories, and the decision to reuse `relates_to` links rather than introduce a new linking mechanism.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new top-level `src/rebar/audit/` package that aggregates across the LLM plan-review subsystem, the code-review subsystem, the ticket store, CLI, and MCP — a novel architectural boundary — but does not mention capturing this design decision in CLAUDE.md, an ADR, or a design doc.
findings.location: Scope section / src/rebar/audit/read.py
findings.suggested_fix: Add a section to CLAUDE.md under an 'Architecture' or 'Audit' heading describing: (1) `src/rebar/audit/read.py` as the canonical read surface for full ticket audit history, (2) its dependency on `relates_to` links written by `emit_code_review_artifact`, and (3) how to extend it for new sidecar types.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability requirement: schema-invalid/skipped events and any caught exceptions in the read path must emit at least a `logging.warning` with the ticket_id and cause, so operators can detect silent data gaps.
findings.finding: No observability (logging or metrics) is specified for failures in the read path (e.g., failed sidecar reads, failed ticket lookups, skipped schema-invalid events). Without instrumentation, operators cannot distinguish a functioning empty result from a silently failing one.
findings.location: Scope / Approach rationale / Compatibility & rollback sections
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'Schema-tolerant readers must `logging.warning(...)` for each skipped/unrecognized record, including the ticket id and the parse error, so gaps are visible in application logs.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `src/rebar/audit/read.py` needs to be a package (anticipating additional sibling modules in a named follow-on) or whether placing it as `src/rebar/audit_read.py` (or `src/rebar/llm/audit_read.py`) better matches the existing one-module-one-file convention used by similar aggregators.
findings.finding: The plan proposes a new `src/rebar/audit/` top-level package containing a single `read.py` module. A new package for one module adds a `__init__.py`, a new import-path namespace (`rebar.audit.read`), and package-discovery overhead with no Rule-of-Three justification: the plan names no second module for this package in the current or follow-on deliverable.
findings.location: Scope section — 'New `src/rebar/audit/read.py`'

findings.finding: The plan names a new MCP `audit_trail` tool but does not enumerate the required edit targets for MCP registration: the tool must be registered via one of the registrar modules (`_mcp_reads.py` is the natural home for a read-only tool) and `mcp_server.py`'s `build_server()` already calls `register_read_tools(mcp, ctx)` — so `_mcp_reads.py` needs editing. Neither `_mcp_reads.py` nor `mcp_server.py` appears in the plan's Scope.
findings.location: Scope section — 'a read-only MCP tool `audit_trail`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: '- `src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py` — register `audit_trail` as a `READ_ONLY`-annotated tool using `@mcp.tool(annotations=_ANN["READ_ONLY"])`, following the pattern of `show_ticket` / `list_tickets`'.

findings.finding: The 'typed dict' returned by `audit_trail` has no specified field schema — field names, nesting structure, and nullability for missing records (e.g. no completion sidecar) are all left to executor discretion, which means the integration test assertion and the MCP tool response shape cannot be written deterministically from the plan alone.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1; Scope — `src/rebar/audit/read.py`
findings.suggested_fix:
Add a schema block to the Scope section, e.g.:
```python
class AuditTrail(TypedDict):
    plan_reviews: list[dict]   # newest-first; empty list if none
    completion: dict | None    # None if no completion record
    code_reviews: list[dict]   # each with its own sidecar history; empty list if no relates_to links
```
and update AC1's oracle to assert key names explicitly.

findings.finding: The plan specifies that audit_trail returns 'a typed dict' but never defines its keys, field names, types, or nesting structure. This is a schema-referenced-but-vocabulary-undefined gap: the return type is the primary contract of the new library function, yet an implementer has no field specification to code against or test against.
findings.location: Scope section: 'returning a typed dict'; AC-1: 'audit_trail(ticket_id) returns the full retained plan-review history, the completion record, and the code reviews linked via relates_to, newest-first'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Return schema' sub-section to Scope or AC-1, e.g.: `{"ticket_id": str, "plan_reviews": list[dict], "completion": {"attestation": dict|None, "sidecar": dict|None}, "code_reviews": list[{"ticket_id": str, "sidecars": list[dict]}]}`. A TypedDict in `read.py` serves the same purpose at implementation time.

findings.finding: The plan specifies no test oracle for failure/empty/invalid paths of the new user-facing `audit_trail` function and its CLI/MCP exposures: (a) ticket_id that does not exist, (b) ticket with zero `relates_to` links (no linked code reviews — the common real-world case), and (c) a sidecar record that is malformed/schema-v1 when the plan explicitly claims schema-tolerance as a delivered property.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (AC1, AC2, AC3) and Scope section ('schema-tolerant readers accept both v1 and v2 records')
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC1: 'Additionally: (a) seed a ticket with no relates_to links and assert `code_reviews` is an empty list; (b) call `audit_trail` on a nonexistent id and assert the return value matches the documented absent-ticket contract (e.g. raises TicketNotFound or returns None with defined shape); (c) seed one v1-format plan-review sidecar alongside a v2 one and assert both appear in the history.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
